item.title: How to enable or disable pop-up blockers on iPhone
item.itunes:title: How to enable or disable pop-up blockers on iPhone
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Interacting with pop-ups during your browsing

When browsing the internet, you will often see ads or other messages pop up on your browser, perhaps as smaller windows or new tabs. These can be legitimate, such as discount offers on shopping sites or informational messages, but pop-ups can also be used for scams and phishing attempts to get you to download malware. Some users prefer to remove the distraction by blocking pop-ups altogether, but you may want to re-enable them if you need to access a specific offer or form.

Related
How to close all your Safari tabs at once on iPhone
Do you have hundreds of open tabs in Safari on your iPhone? Stop closing them one-by-one and do this instead.

Pop-up blocking on iPhone

Most modern browsers have a way to block or allow pop-ups on your desktop computer, and the same is true on an iPhone. Safari is the default browser on iPhone, but other browser apps also give you control over your pop-up experience. The method will be a little different depending on which browser you are using on your phone, but likely similar.

Instructions to block pop-ups on Safari on your iPhone

  1. Open Settings > Safari.
  2. Scroll down to the section titled General.
  3. Use the Block Pop-ups slider to enable or disable blocking.
  4. You will need to reload any pages you were browsing for the setting to take effect.

Instructions to block pop-ups on Chrome on your iPhone

  1. Open the Chrome browser app.
  2. Tap the ... icon at the bottom of your window.
  3. Scroll to the right and tap on the Settings icon.
  4. Tap on Content Settings > Block Pop-ups.
  5. Use the slider to enable or disable pop-up blocking.
  6. You will need to reload any pages you were browsing for the setting to take effect.

Chrome allows you to enable pop-ups for a specific site, even when they are being blocked in general. A message saying "Pop-ups blocked" will be shown at the bottom of your window when a pop-up is currently being blocked. If desired, you can tap on that message and choose to allow pop-ups from that site.

Taking control over your pop-up experience

Whether or not you want to allow pop-ups during your browsing is a personal preference, so iPhone browsers allow you to have control over that feature. You are able to easily change this setting based on your needs for a particular site as well. If you find pop-ups distracting, now you know how to take action to block them.

item.title: Standout apps that will increase your efficiency in Windows
item.itunes:title: Standout apps that will increase your efficiency in Windows
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Microsoft To Do offers a simple solution to task management chaos with its clean interface and synchronization with Office 365 tasks.
  • Microsoft Whiteboard provides a digital space for visual collaboration and project planning with real-time features and multimedia support.
  • QuickLook brings MacBook-like file preview features to Windows, saving time and enhancing productivity with its wide range of supported formats.

Staying productive with Windows takes more than determination, especially when you consider life's numerous distractions. This guide will spotlight the best productivity apps for Windows, each chosen to ramp up your efficiency at work and in life. These task management and team collaboration programs will be your secret to boosting productivity. Anyone can use these apps — professionals, students, or just casual users — especially if they already use Windows. Let's explore how these apps can reshape your daily routine into something more streamlined, efficient, and productive.

10 Microsoft To Do

Have you ever felt overwhelmed by a jumble of tasks and reminders scribbled on sticky notes or scattered across various apps? Microsoft To Do is the perfect way to bring some order to that chaos. This neat little app from Microsoft is all about making task management as straightforward as possible. The beauty of Microsoft To Do lies in its simplicity. It offers a clean, no-fuss interface that lets you jot down tasks, set deadlines, and categorize them into personalized lists.

Microsoft To Do wins over users with more than just a neat interface; the app also fits snugly into the Microsoft ecosystem. If you already use Office 365, To Do syncs smoothly with your Outlook tasks to become a natural extension. Most importantly, Microsoft To Do sticks with you when you're on your laptop, tablet, or phone, ensuring your to-dos are always at hand.

Microsoft To Do also has plenty of customization options. You can color code your lists or pick themes that suit your mood to make even the mundane task of managing tasks a bit more fun. However, To Do isn't just for professionals who need to juggle projects; it's equally handy for students planning study schedules, or really anyone looking to streamline their day-to-day tasks. In a nutshell, Microsoft To Do takes the hassle out of keeping track of your tasks, and that’s something we could all use a little help with.

Microsoft To Do
See at Microsoft Store See at Microsoft

9 Microsoft Whiteboard

Ever had a brainstorming session where you wished you had a giant canvas to capture everything? Enter Microsoft Whiteboard. This application lets you visually map out all of your ideas with friends and co-workers, whether you are in the same room or scattered around the globe.

Microsoft Whiteboard excels in its simplicity and ability to mimic the experience of using a physical whiteboard, but the app also adds the benefits of digital communication technology. Not only can you write and draw on the digital whiteboard, you can also incorporate multimedia elements. This real-time collaboration feature also enables team members to contribute simultaneously.

This app's versatility extends beyond creative fields. Microsoft Whiteboard can help you plan out projects, organize complex concepts, and even educate students. Whatever your needs, Whiteboard adapts to it, especially if you use the Microsoft ecosystem.

Microsoft Whiteboard
See at Microsoft Store See at Microsoft

8 QuickLook

QuickLook offers a simple yet powerful solution for Windows users. This nifty app brings MacBook-like features to the Windows platform, such as the ability to preview files with just a press of a button. QuickLook also streamlines the process of sifting through files, as you can skim a file's contents without opening it in a dedicated program.

One of QuickLook's main strengths is its wide range of supported file formats. Documents, photos, videos, and PDFs are all accepted, saving valuable time that would otherwise be spent waiting for different applications to load and open the files. QuickLook's universal format functionality is ideal for professionals who regularly work with disparate file formats and need to quickly assess and organize their contents.

Another noteworthy aspect of QuickLook is its smooth integration into the Windows environment. The app enhances the user experience without disrupting the familiar Windows workflow, which makes QuickLook almost feel like a native Windows feature. In a digital workspace where time and ease of access are paramount, QuickLook stands out as a valuable tool.

QuickLook
See at Microsoft Store

7 Toggl

Toggl offers a streamlined approach to time management that appeals to anyone who needs to meticulously track their time, including freelancers, consultants, and project managers. The app's user-friendly interface lets you effortlessly calculate how much time you have spent on tasks and projects, which is a necessity for effective billing and productivity management.

While Toggl offers many features, it focuses on detailed reporting. You gain valuable insights into how you allocate your time, which helps you identify problem areas where efficiency could be improved. This level of detail aids in workload management and ensures that you devote enough time to high-priority tasks.

Compatibility across different platforms is another of Toggl's strong suits. The program integrates well with other productivity tools, which lets you seamlessly track your experience whether you're at your desk or on the move. Toggl's commitment to simplicity and actionable data analysis makes it a powerful tool for anyone looking to optimize time management and productivity.

Toggl Track
See at Toggl

6 Grammarly

Grammarly is more than just a spell-checker. The service offers a comprehensive tool that helps you refine your writing by checking for grammar, punctuation, style, and even tone. Grammarly has redefined writing assistance and is invaluable for anyone who writes regularly, whether for professional reports, academic papers, emails, or social media posts.

Grammarly sets itself apart from rival tools with advanced algorithms that detect errors and suggest context-specific corrections and improvements. This feature is particularly beneficial for non-native English speakers, as it helps level up their writing to professional standards. Additionally, Grammarly offers explanations for its corrections, which aids in the learning process and improves users' writing skills over time.

The app integrates into web browsers, word processors, and email clients, which ensures it is always available to help you maintain a high standard of writing across all communications. By assisting in clear, error-free, and effective writing, Grammarly has become an essential tool for anyone looking to improve their writing skills.

Grammarly

Grammarly is a popular spell-checking and grammar extension for Chrome.

See at Chrome Web Store

5 Slack

Slack has established itself as an indispensable tool that transforms how team members interact and communicate with one another digitally. The app is essential for fast-paced business environments because it streamlines instant messaging and collaboration tasks. Slack distinguishes itself from rival services with its clever channel-based approach, so that teams can segregate discussions into specific topics or projects. This organization prevents conversation clutter and ensures relevant and focused communication, a key factor for efficiency in any team.

Moreover, Slack provides extensive integration with a multitude of third-party applications, which turns it into a central hub for workplace activities. This integration harmonizes various tools and services, simplifying workflows and reducing the need to switch between different apps. Additionally, Slack enhances overall team productivity by offering features such as file sharing, voice and video calling, and customizable notifications. The app boasts an intuitive interface and is compatible with a wide range of devices, so team members can communicate and collaborate seamlessly regardless of location and platform.

Slack
See at Microsoft Store See at Slack

4 Obsidian

Obsidian is a bit like a digital notebook that does more than just store your notes. This app is extremely helpful for anyone who needs to organize complex information. Obsidian uses Markdown to format text, and you can link your notes together to create a network that indicates how your ideas relate to each other. You can build your own database like a mind-map of information.

Obsidian also offers plenty of customization options. You can add plugins or change the theme to personalize your workspace. And since your notes are stored locally on your device, you have full control over them. Overall, Obsidian is a straightforward, powerful tool for anyone who wants to visualize the bigger picture in their notes. Make the connections between your ideas in a creative but organized way.

Obsidian

Obsidian is a feature-rich note-taking app that's available on different platforms and offers a neat and clean interface. It's also free-to-use for individuals. 

Download Obsidian

3 One Calendar

One Calendar is a gem for anyone who has to juggle multiple calendars. This app collects all your appointments from platforms such as Google Calendar and Outlook under one roof, streamlining your schedules. You get a unified view of all your commitments, from work meetings to personal appointments, in a single interface. This consolidation helps you avoid scheduling conflicts and makes sure you're always on top of your plans.

However, One Calendar is not just about viewing calendars. You can customize the look and feel of the app to fit your preferences, which not only makes One Calendar feel more personalized but can also help it appear less cluttered. One Calendar is a straightforward, no-nonsense way to keep your schedules in check, all in one place. Plus, it's a great choice for anyone who needs to keep an eye on their schedule from different platforms.

One Calendar
See at Microsoft Store

2 ShareX

ShareX is a standout tool for anyone who needs to frequently capture and share screenshots or screen recordings. The app is versatile and brimming with features, yet it doesn't cost a dime. ShareX is especially popular among professionals who need to create tutorials, provide feedback, or simply share what's on their screen.

One of the tool's main hooks is its range of capture options. You can easily record a full screen, a specific window, or even a scrolling capture. The program also offers a variety of post-capture actions, such as the ability to add annotations and watermarks, or upload videos to different platforms. ShareX streamlines the whole process from start to finish.

ShareX also includes a suite of tools that go beyond basic screen capturing. You can edit and customize your videos with a color picker and ruler, automate workflows with configurable hotkeys, and even create your own GIFs. Overall, ShareX is an excellent tool for any workplace that relies on videos.

sharex

ShareX is a screenshot and screen recording tool for all kinds of purposes. It can record all or part of your screen, and it even works with games. It also provides tools for easy sharing by uploading files to the internet.

See at ShareX See at Microsoft

1 Notion

Notion unifies note-taking, task management, and database functionality into one interface to cut down the need for multiple apps. This platform is versatile and appeals to a broad audience, from individuals organizing personal tasks to teams collaborating on complex projects.

One of Notion's key attributes is its modular design, which empowers users to customize their digital workspace. Whether you're drafting elaborate notes, creating dynamic project boards, or managing schedules, Notion adapts effortlessly. Moreover, Notion boasts collaborative functionalities such as shared workspaces and simultaneous team document editing. These features are ideal for teams, especially when members work remotely. Overall, Notion establishes itself as a comprehensive solution for managing diverse tasks and information thanks to its tools and integration capabilities.

Notion
See at Notion

Tools of the trade

We've taken a good look at some standout apps that can sharpen your productivity in Windows. Remember, it's not about cluttering your desktop with a ton of apps, but instead finding the ones that resonate with you and your workflow. You might be pleasantly surprised, or even astonished, by how some of these can transform your workdays. From turning your scattered thoughts into well-mapped treasure troves via Obsidian, to keeping tabs on your disparate schedules via One Calendar, each app has its own way of smoothing out the kinks in your day-to-day life.

Think of these programs as your go-to crew for getting things done. Once you've tried a few, you will wonder how you ever lived without them. Give some a whirl, mix and match them to fit your workflow, and watch these tools turn your average day into a productivity powerhouse. After all, it's all about working smarter, not harder.

See at Microsoft Store See at Microsoft

9 Microsoft Whiteboard

Ever had a brainstorming session where you wished you had a giant canvas to capture everything? Enter Microsoft Whiteboard. This application lets you visually map out all of your ideas with friends and co-workers, whether you are in the same room or scattered around the globe.

Microsoft Whiteboard excels in its simplicity and ability to mimic the experience of using a physical whiteboard, but the app also adds the benefits of digital communication technology. Not only can you write and draw on the digital whiteboard, you can also incorporate multimedia elements. This real-time collaboration feature also enables team members to contribute simultaneously.

This app's versatility extends beyond creative fields. Microsoft Whiteboard can help you plan out projects, organize complex concepts, and even educate students. Whatever your needs, Whiteboard adapts to it, especially if you use the Microsoft ecosystem.

Microsoft Whiteboard
See at Microsoft Store See at Microsoft

8 QuickLook

QuickLook offers a simple yet powerful solution for Windows users. This nifty app brings MacBook-like features to the Windows platform, such as the ability to preview files with just a press of a button. QuickLook also streamlines the process of sifting through files, as you can skim a file's contents without opening it in a dedicated program.

One of QuickLook's main strengths is its wide range of supported file formats. Documents, photos, videos, and PDFs are all accepted, saving valuable time that would otherwise be spent waiting for different applications to load and open the files. QuickLook's universal format functionality is ideal for professionals who regularly work with disparate file formats and need to quickly assess and organize their contents.

Another noteworthy aspect of QuickLook is its smooth integration into the Windows environment. The app enhances the user experience without disrupting the familiar Windows workflow, which makes QuickLook almost feel like a native Windows feature. In a digital workspace where time and ease of access are paramount, QuickLook stands out as a valuable tool.

QuickLook
See at Microsoft Store

7 Toggl

Toggl offers a streamlined approach to time management that appeals to anyone who needs to meticulously track their time, including freelancers, consultants, and project managers. The app's user-friendly interface lets you effortlessly calculate how much time you have spent on tasks and projects, which is a necessity for effective billing and productivity management.

While Toggl offers many features, it focuses on detailed reporting. You gain valuable insights into how you allocate your time, which helps you identify problem areas where efficiency could be improved. This level of detail aids in workload management and ensures that you devote enough time to high-priority tasks.

Compatibility across different platforms is another of Toggl's strong suits. The program integrates well with other productivity tools, which lets you seamlessly track your experience whether you're at your desk or on the move. Toggl's commitment to simplicity and actionable data analysis makes it a powerful tool for anyone looking to optimize time management and productivity.

Toggl Track
See at Toggl

6 Grammarly

Grammarly is more than just a spell-checker. The service offers a comprehensive tool that helps you refine your writing by checking for grammar, punctuation, style, and even tone. Grammarly has redefined writing assistance and is invaluable for anyone who writes regularly, whether for professional reports, academic papers, emails, or social media posts.

Grammarly sets itself apart from rival tools with advanced algorithms that detect errors and suggest context-specific corrections and improvements. This feature is particularly beneficial for non-native English speakers, as it helps level up their writing to professional standards. Additionally, Grammarly offers explanations for its corrections, which aids in the learning process and improves users' writing skills over time.

The app integrates into web browsers, word processors, and email clients, which ensures it is always available to help you maintain a high standard of writing across all communications. By assisting in clear, error-free, and effective writing, Grammarly has become an essential tool for anyone looking to improve their writing skills.

Grammarly

Grammarly is a popular spell-checking and grammar extension for Chrome.

See at Chrome Web Store

5 Slack

Slack has established itself as an indispensable tool that transforms how team members interact and communicate with one another digitally. The app is essential for fast-paced business environments because it streamlines instant messaging and collaboration tasks. Slack distinguishes itself from rival services with its clever channel-based approach, so that teams can segregate discussions into specific topics or projects. This organization prevents conversation clutter and ensures relevant and focused communication, a key factor for efficiency in any team.

Moreover, Slack provides extensive integration with a multitude of third-party applications, which turns it into a central hub for workplace activities. This integration harmonizes various tools and services, simplifying workflows and reducing the need to switch between different apps. Additionally, Slack enhances overall team productivity by offering features such as file sharing, voice and video calling, and customizable notifications. The app boasts an intuitive interface and is compatible with a wide range of devices, so team members can communicate and collaborate seamlessly regardless of location and platform.

Slack
See at Microsoft Store See at Slack

4 Obsidian

Obsidian is a bit like a digital notebook that does more than just store your notes. This app is extremely helpful for anyone who needs to organize complex information. Obsidian uses Markdown to format text, and you can link your notes together to create a network that indicates how your ideas relate to each other. You can build your own database like a mind-map of information.

Obsidian also offers plenty of customization options. You can add plugins or change the theme to personalize your workspace. And since your notes are stored locally on your device, you have full control over them. Overall, Obsidian is a straightforward, powerful tool for anyone who wants to visualize the bigger picture in their notes. Make the connections between your ideas in a creative but organized way.

Obsidian

Obsidian is a feature-rich note-taking app that's available on different platforms and offers a neat and clean interface. It's also free-to-use for individuals. 

Download Obsidian

3 One Calendar

One Calendar is a gem for anyone who has to juggle multiple calendars. This app collects all your appointments from platforms such as Google Calendar and Outlook under one roof, streamlining your schedules. You get a unified view of all your commitments, from work meetings to personal appointments, in a single interface. This consolidation helps you avoid scheduling conflicts and makes sure you're always on top of your plans.

However, One Calendar is not just about viewing calendars. You can customize the look and feel of the app to fit your preferences, which not only makes One Calendar feel more personalized but can also help it appear less cluttered. One Calendar is a straightforward, no-nonsense way to keep your schedules in check, all in one place. Plus, it's a great choice for anyone who needs to keep an eye on their schedule from different platforms.

One Calendar
See at Microsoft Store

2 ShareX

ShareX is a standout tool for anyone who needs to frequently capture and share screenshots or screen recordings. The app is versatile and brimming with features, yet it doesn't cost a dime. ShareX is especially popular among professionals who need to create tutorials, provide feedback, or simply share what's on their screen.

One of the tool's main hooks is its range of capture options. You can easily record a full screen, a specific window, or even a scrolling capture. The program also offers a variety of post-capture actions, such as the ability to add annotations and watermarks, or upload videos to different platforms. ShareX streamlines the whole process from start to finish.

ShareX also includes a suite of tools that go beyond basic screen capturing. You can edit and customize your videos with a color picker and ruler, automate workflows with configurable hotkeys, and even create your own GIFs. Overall, ShareX is an excellent tool for any workplace that relies on videos.

sharex

ShareX is a screenshot and screen recording tool for all kinds of purposes. It can record all or part of your screen, and it even works with games. It also provides tools for easy sharing by uploading files to the internet.

See at ShareX See at Microsoft

1 Notion

Notion unifies note-taking, task management, and database functionality into one interface to cut down the need for multiple apps. This platform is versatile and appeals to a broad audience, from individuals organizing personal tasks to teams collaborating on complex projects.

One of Notion's key attributes is its modular design, which empowers users to customize their digital workspace. Whether you're drafting elaborate notes, creating dynamic project boards, or managing schedules, Notion adapts effortlessly. Moreover, Notion boasts collaborative functionalities such as shared workspaces and simultaneous team document editing. These features are ideal for teams, especially when members work remotely. Overall, Notion establishes itself as a comprehensive solution for managing diverse tasks and information thanks to its tools and integration capabilities.

Notion
See at Notion

Tools of the trade

We've taken a good look at some standout apps that can sharpen your productivity in Windows. Remember, it's not about cluttering your desktop with a ton of apps, but instead finding the ones that resonate with you and your workflow. You might be pleasantly surprised, or even astonished, by how some of these can transform your workdays. From turning your scattered thoughts into well-mapped treasure troves via Obsidian, to keeping tabs on your disparate schedules via One Calendar, each app has its own way of smoothing out the kinks in your day-to-day life.

Think of these programs as your go-to crew for getting things done. Once you've tried a few, you will wonder how you ever lived without them. Give some a whirl, mix and match them to fit your workflow, and watch these tools turn your average day into a productivity powerhouse. After all, it's all about working smarter, not harder.

item.title: Best gaming monitors in 2024
item.itunes:title: Best gaming monitors in 2024
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
You have finally managed to buy all the components for your new gaming rig, including a new graphics card. I hope you still have some money left in the budget because now it's time to complete your rig with one of the best gaming monitors. Luckily, there's no shortage of great monitors on the market, so you can pick up the one you want based on the GPU you bought.

There are a few things to consider while shopping for gaming monitors. The one-size-fits-all approach doesn't work here because each user has a different preference. OLED and QD-OLED are taking over as the main panel technology for new gaming monitors, but some users will prefer IPS or VA as fears of OLED burn-in still linger. While some users want a high-refresh-rate panel, others may want to buy a high-resolution monitor for 4K gaming. And still other people give more importance to extra technologies like HDMI 2.1. So, not all of these recommendations might suit your unique needs, but these monitors are worth getting.

  • Source: Asus
    Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM
    Best overall gaming monitor
    $889 at B&H
  • Alienware AW3225QF
    Best 4K gaming monitor
    $1200 at Dell
  • Acer Predator X27U
    Best 1440p monitor
    $730 at Amazon
  • Source: Asus
    Asus ROG Strix XG27ACS
    Best budget 1440p gaming monitor
    $290 at Amazon
  • Source: Dell
    Dell Alienware AW2523HF
    Best 1080p gaming monitor
    $300 at Amazon
  • LG 24GN600-B UltraGear Gaming Monitor
    Best budget 1080p gaming monitor
    $150 at Amazon
  • Source: Dell
    Alienware AW3423DW Gaming Monitor
    Best ultrawide gaming monitor
    $1100 at Amazon
  • Source: Gigabyte
    Gigabyte Aorus FV43U
    Best large gaming monitor
    $710 at Amazon
  • Samsung Odyssey Neo G8 4K UHD Curved Gaming Monitor
    Best gaming monitor with HDMI 2.1
    $847 at Amazon

These are the best gaming monitors in 2024

Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM
Best overall gaming monitor

A monitor with everything you could ask for

The ASUS ROG Swift OLED PG27AQDM is the best gaming monitor of 2024, featuring a beautiful OLED panel with a wide range of brightness and individual brightness control settings for each pixel. It also features a sharp 1440p resolution and a super-fast 240Hz refresh rate that can be boosted to 280Hz.

Pros
  • 1440p, 240Hz refresh rate (via DisplayPort)
  • FreeSync Premium Pro and G-Sync Compatible
  • WOLED panel
Cons
  • Expensive and probably overkill for most people
  • Use of HDMI limits refresh rate to 120Hz
$900 at Amazon $889 at B&H $899 at Asus

The ASUS ROG Swift OLED PG27AQDM is one of the best gaming monitors in 2024, thanks to its stunning OLED panel, ultra-fast response time, and super-speedy refresh rate. The OLED panel delivers a spectacular 1440p resolution with deep blacks, vivid colors, and wide viewing angles. The 2560 x 1440 resolution on a 27-inch OLED panel delivers sharp details and a peak brightness of 1000 nits. HDR content looks stunning on the PG27AQDM. The anti-glare coating is great, and the included stand has a 1/4-inch thread on top for mounting a heavier camera, something content creators will appreciate.

Related
Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM review: For those who crave a gorgeous OLED and high refresh rates for gaming
Among the best OLEDs you can buy.

It's worth mentioning that the HDMI 2.0 ports limit this monitor to a refresh rate of 120Hz, so you'll want to use the DisplayPort 1.4 input to get the 240Hz refresh rate the panel is capable of. The PG27AQDM is great for high visual quality and high framerate gaming. The PG27AQDM features a 0.03ms response time, making it one of the fastest gaming monitors on the market. The monitor can handle fast-paced games without ghosting, blurring, or graphical smearing. That's supported with G-Sync and FreeSync compatibility for blur-free gaming.

The PG27AQDM is an expensive monitor with a $900 MSRP. However, if you are building a mid-range to high-end gaming computer, the PG27AQDM is one of the best monitors thanks to its beautiful OLED panel, 240Hz refresh rate, and excellent HDR support with 1,000 nits of peak brightness.

Alienware AW3225QF
Best 4K gaming monitor

A high-end display best paired with the RTX 4090 and other flagship cards

The Alienware AW3225QF is a powerhouse of gaming performance, with a 32-inch QD-OLED panel with 4K resolution that supports a speedy 240Hz refresh rate. It also supports Dolby Vision, which is a nice bonus for watching Netflix and other streaming services that support the format.

Pros
  • 4K resolution QD-OLED panel with 240Hz refresh rate
  • G-Sync Compatible and VESA AdaptiveSync Certified
  • Excellent HDR performance
Cons
  • Very expensive
$1200 at Dell $1200 at Best Buy

Powerful new graphics cards like the RTX 4090 enable 4K gaming as the norm. If you have one of these powerful GPUs and want to pair it with a high-end 4K display, then I recommend picking up the Alienware AW322QF. It's one of the first 4K QD-OLED panels on the market with a blisteringly fast 240Hz refresh rate and will likely hold up as one of the best gaming monitors through the next generation or two of graphics cards.

Related
Dell Alienware AW3225QF monitor review: Ahead of the curve
The first 4K 32-inch QD-OLED made by one of the most renowned PC monitor makers

While there are more OLED panels on the market now, QD-OLED is still my preferred panel type. That's because it has better color accuracy than WOLED, even if it can't quite manage the same brightness levels. This Alienware monitor has a subtle curve, which isn't strictly necessary on a 32-inch panel, but we found that it distorted screen reflections a bit so that they didn't affect the image as much. This panel is a third-generation QD-OLED, which has a different subpixel arrangement that reduces fringing around horizontal edges, like on text, which is a welcome change.

That reduced fringing will also benefit UI elements in games, and the rest of the panel is superb for gaming use. The 4K resolution and 240Hz refresh rate combine for a crisp, smooth picture, and support for both G-Sync and VESA AdaptiveSync means tear-free gaming. It supports Dolby Vision in supported games and apps and also HDR 400 TrueBlack and HDR Peak 1000 modes for HDR. We found that the panel has a warmer, punchy color balance in SDR on our review unit, which will please most users but may cause issues with color-sensitive work. Its HDR performance is among the best panels we've tested and is a worthy pairing with high-end graphics cards.

Acer Predator X27U
Best 1440p monitor

A high-end 1440p display for high-end PCs

$730 $798 Save $68

The Acer Predator X27U is a premium gaming monitor with stunning visuals, smooth performance, and a zero-frame design. It features a 27-inch OLED panel, a 1440p resolution, 240Hz refresh rate, and HDR 1000 support, delivering lifelike colors.

Pros
  • 1,000 nits peak brightness
  • 1440p OLED panel with 240Hz refresh rate
  • FreeSync Premium Pro
Cons
  • Expensive
  • Poor speaker quality
  • No HDMI 2.1
$730 at Amazon $800 at Acer

While 4K gaming is no longer a distant dream for gamers, most are still leaning towards 1440p gaming thanks to its lower price, lower graphical demand, and higher refresh rate. If you want a great OLED 1440p gaming monitor, check out Acer's Predator X27U.

The Predator X27U sports a 27-inch OLED panel with a 240Hz refresh rate and support for AMD FreeSync Premium Pro. The display has a peak brightness of 1000 nits, providing an excellent OLED gaming experience. Colors appear bright and vivid in HDR content, and are true to realistic coloration.

The Zero-frame design with thin bezels provides an expansive space to game or multitask on, and there's a wide range of connectivity options, whether you prefer to use HDMI, DisplayPort 1.4, or DisplayPort over USB Type-C.

Asus ROG Strix XG27ACS
Best budget 1440p gaming monitor

A cost-effective 1440p gaming monitor

The Asus ROG Strix XG27ACS is a cost-effective way to add a gorgeous, speedy gaming monitor to your setup. The IPS panel might not have stellar HDR performance, but it's perfectly fine for SDR gaming.

Pros
  • 1440p, 180Hz IPS display
  • FreeSync and G-Sync Compatible
  • Well-calibrated from the factory
Cons
  • Weak HDR performance
$290 at Amazon $300 at Best Buy $320 at Newegg

With 1440p still being the sweet spot for PC gaming, it's no surprise to see a 27-inch, 1440p monitor take the budget crown here. The Asus ROG Strix XG27ACS uses a 27-inch IPS panel that's overclocked to a 180Hz refresh rate. That means it's perfect for playing games at high framerates, and support for both AMD FreeSync and Nvidia G-Sync means buttery smooth frames no matter what rate they come at.

Related
Asus ROG Strix XG27ACS review: A gorgeous budget-friendly 1440p gaming monitor
This is all you need for immersive PC gaming in the evenings.

With a fairly subdued gamer styling, this monitor wouldn't look out of place on an office desk. It's got a mostly frameless front with a small bezel on the bottom edge, and comes with a sturdy, adjustable stand. It also features USB-C with power delivery, alongside the DisplayPort and HDMI inputs, which is a rarity on any budget monitor.

The big surprise when we reviewed this monitor was how accurate the factory calibration was. It covered 100% of sRGB, 82% of Adobe RGB, and 91% of DCI P3, which is great for any IPS panel, let alone at this price point. It's worth mentioning that the HDR 400 certification on this panel won't pop as much as on an OLED, mainly because IPS has a very noticeable glow on dark areas. Still, we are talking about a $300 budget monitor here. Asus spent their development budget wisely here, with the resolution, color accuracy, and refresh rate necessary to give you a capable monitor at an affordable price.

Dell Alienware AW2523HF
Best 1080p gaming monitor

The best if you need the highest refresh rate possible

$300 $350 Save $50

The Alienware AW2523HF from Dell is an excellent esport monitor with a 1080p resolution and a super-fast 360Hz refresh rate. This is an ideal monitor for competitive first-person shooters, and is the best option if you want to use max settings and speed.

Pros
  • 360Hz refresh rate
  • Support for AMD FreeSync
  • 25-inch size is a good fit for 1080p
Cons
  • Extremely expensive for a 1080p monitor
  • High refresh rate might not be as important as better resolution
$300 at Amazon $300 at Best Buy

You might think 1080p monitors have been relegated to the low-end, budget part of the market, but that's not entirely true. There's actually a small segment of top-end 1080p gaming monitors that have the highest refresh rates you can get, much higher than the refresh rates on even the best 1440p and 4K panels. Of all the 360Hz monitors you can buy today, Dell's Alienware AW2523HF is the best overall, though it will cost you.

Related
Alienware AW2725DF 27-inch QD-OLED review: Combining great color accuracy with 360Hz refresh rates
There are plenty of high refresh rate or color-accurate OLED gaming monitors out there, but it's rare to see both characteristics on the same display.

The AW2523HF goes for the standard minimalist look many monitors have these days: thin bezels at the sides and top, slightly thicker chin at the bottom, and an all-black plastic theme. It's an unassuming monitor that certainly doesn't look eye-catching, although that's not really a problem when it's the panel that's supposed to look good and not what surrounds it.

Speaking of the panel, it measures 25 inches across and is rated at 360Hz. The AW2523HF comes with support for AMD FreeSync and is G-Sync Compatible, which pretty much has to be on a monitor that sells itself on its refresh rate. Its peak brightness of around 400 nits is decent enough, though out-of-the-box reviews indicate that its color accuracy isn't amazing, and it doesn't have any HDR certification either.

Although it's our top pick in this segment, Dell's Alienware AW2523HF is undeniably in a niche within a niche. A 1080p monitor, even with a 360Hz refresh rate, is expensive at $300+. However, the AW2523HF is really made for games like Valorant and Overwatch, and not so much for gaming in general. If you have the money and don't plan on playing all your games at over 300FPS, you should probably consider HP's X27q or Acer's Nitro XV272U, which are 1440p gaming monitors that cost less.

LG 24GN600-B UltraGear Gaming Monitor
Best budget 1080p gaming monitor

Cheap but still good for gaming

$150 $200 Save $50

LG's 24GN600-B UltaGear is an excellent entry-level gaming monitor. Measuring at 24-inches, this 1080p monitor has a refresh rate of 144 Hz and FreeSync Premium support for a tear-free gaming experience. While it's not the biggest monitor, it's one of the best and most affordable 1080p gaming monitors.

Pros
  • 1080p IPS display
  • 144Hz refresh rate
  • AMD FreeSync Premium
Cons
  • No explicit support for G-Sync (though it should still function)
$150 at Amazon $150 at LG

In recent years, entry-level gaming monitors have gotten better while prices remain about as low as they've always been. It's not uncommon to find a 1080p display with a high refresh rate for less than $200, but LG's 24GN600-B UltraGear manages to stand out from the crowd by offering tons of features and impressive specs for just under $200 (with frequent sales bringing it under $150).

From the front, the 24GN600-B is a pretty normal-looking 24-inch monitor. It has a thin, pure black bezel made of plastic decorated only by the LG logo. On the back, however, this monitor can only be described as gamer-y thanks to its purely decorative wheel-like image with red accents. The back of the monitor stand is also pure red. It's kind of weird that all the flashy esthetics are put in the one area where you're the least likely to be looking, but that might work out if you prefer the basic appearance of this monitor from the front anyway.

On a technical level, the 24GN600-B has quite an impressive set of features. 1080p and a 144Hz refresh rate is nothing special these days, but this is also an IPS display with AMD FreeSync Premium. That basically ensures good color accuracy (LG says this monitor hits 99% of the sRGB color space) and good enough latency for gaming. For $150, you're getting a high refresh rate, decent color accuracy, and limited to no screen tearing, no matter what GPU you're using, since all three major vendors support FreeSync. It's hard to ask for anything more.

There are other contenders in the sub-$200 segment, but LG's 24GN600-B UltraGear checks off the most boxes for the least amount of money. Asus's TUF Gaming VG27VH1B is one such alternative, which bumps up the screen size to 27 inches, the refresh rate up to 165Hz, and it has a curved display. However, the 24GN600-B goes on sale more frequently and for lower prices than the VG27VH1B, which makes it a generally better deal as long as you can wait for LG's monitor to hit that expected $150 price point.

Alienware AW3423DW Gaming Monitor
Best ultrawide gaming monitor

A top-of-the-line ultrawide display for gaming

The Alienware AW3423DW Gaming Monitor is one of the best 1440p ultra-wide monitors, featuring a 1440p resolution, 175Hz refresh rate, and support for both Nvidia G-Sync and AMD FreeSync, so you'll have a tear-free experience no matter which GPU you have.

Pros
  • 1440p and 175Hz refresh rate
  • G-Sync and FreeSync support
  • Fantastic HDR experience
Cons
  • Extremely expensive
$1100 at Amazon $1100 at Dell

Ultrawide monitors are the realm for gamers who want to make full use of their peripheral vision. Gaming at an ultrawide resolution can be buggy at times and requires more graphical horsepower than the typical 16:9 aspect ratio, but the visual experience has proven to be worth those issues. Of all the ultrawide monitors out there, Dell's Alienware AW3423DW is certainly one of the best, though it's not exactly easy on the wallet.

From the front, the AW3423DW presents its thin bezels and almost pure black design, making it look like a pretty normal monitor. On the back, however, there's quite a bit of visual flair; the black largely gives way to white, and there's some RGB around where the monitor stand meets the monitor itself. However, what a monitor looks like from the back isn't always important, especially if you have this monitor up against a wall or something.

The AW3423DW has a top-end Quantum Dot OLED 1440p panel with a 175Hz refresh rate. This is also an 1800R curved display, which is sometimes not desirable on typical gaming monitors but is often very appreciated on ultrawide models like this one. The AW3423DW supports G-Sync (not G-Sync Compatible but actual G-Sync) and AMD FreeSync, which means no screen tearing in games, no matter what GPU you have. One weakness of this monitor is its relatively low SDR brightness of 250 nits, but in HDR content, the brightness is allowed to hit values closer to 1000 nits. It's worth mentioning that firmware updates are now possible to do at home by the user, whereas they used to require sending the monitor back to Dell to be updated.

Dell's Alienware AW3423DW is undeniably one of the best ultrawide gaming monitors out there, but it's also one of the most expensive monitors overall. Cheaper alternatives include Samsung's SJ55W, which is also 1440p and $300 but is limited to 75Hz, Gigabyte's GG34WQC A, which offers 1440p and 144Hz for $400, and Dell's non-Alienware S3422DWG, which is also around $400 for a 1440p, 144Hz panel. These displays are all, of course, inferior in quality to the AW3423DW, which is the best as long as you can afford it.

Gigabyte Aorus FV43U
Best large gaming monitor

Great for the living room

$710 $750 Save $40

Gigabyte's Aorus FV43U is a TV-sized monitor measuring 40-inches in a VA panel, and provides a crisp 4K resolution, excellent contrast, and a fast 144Hz refresh rate. If you are looking for a large format monitor, the Gigabyte AORUS FV43U is the best without breaking the bank.

Pros
  • 43 inches
  • 4K resolution
  • 144Hz refresh rate
Cons
  • VA panels are a bit outdated
$710 at Amazon

While most people don't lean towards buying monitors bigger than 32 inches, there are people who like huge monitors for their gaming setups. If you are one of them, then you'd be interested in checking out the Gigabyte Aorus FV43U. This monitor is essentially a TV replacement, packing a massive 43-inch panel; however, it doesn't have typical TV functions like channel switching, and it doesn't even come with a remote. But you can sit close and enjoy content or play games on it. It also has a good amount of height thanks to the 16:9 aspect ratio, so it's a very different experience to using an ultrawide.

The Gigabyte Aorus FV43U comes with a 43-inch 4K VA with a 144Hz refresh rate and 1ms response time, which means it's great for gaming. In addition to that, the Gigabyte Aorus FV43U also has support for FreeSync, so you can get rid of those nasty tears on AMD Radeon-powered systems, though it should also work with Nvidia GPUs with some manual driver option selections.

In terms of image quality, the Gigabyte Aorus FV43U handles things very well too. It offers 300 nits of brightness typically and a 4000:1 contrast ratio, which makes up for some of the typical weaknesses of a VA display. It also comes with support for DisplayHDR 1000 and HDR10 certification. Those who are looking to use this monitor with the next-generation consoles, including Xbox Series X, S, and PS5, will also have a great time as it sports HDMI 2.1 ports.

If you're focused on size or a display that has great gaming features for the living room, then Gigabyte's Aorus FV43U would be an awesome choice. There aren't a ton of TV-sized gaming monitors out there to begin with, so the FV43U doesn't necessarily win by default and also has lots of things going for it like its decently high 144Hz refresh rate and 4K resolution.

Samsung Odyssey Neo G8 4K UHD Curved Gaming Monitor
Best gaming monitor with HDMI 2.1

If you need HDMI 2.1 in particular

$847 $1300 Save $453

Samsung's 32-inch Odyssey Neo G8 4K UHD Curved Gaming Monitor is one of the best monitor's on the market if you can afford it. It features multiple DisplayPort and HDMI 2.1 ports so that you can enjoy not only PC gaming but also PlayStation 5 and Xbox Series X gaming on this monitor.

Pros
  • Has HDMI 2.1
  • 4K VA panel
  • 144Hz refresh rate with FreeSync Premium
Cons
  • Fairly expensive
$847 at Amazon $1300 at Samsung $900 at Newegg $1300 at Best Buy

If you're looking for a monitor with HDMI 2.1 for PlayStation 5 and Xbox Series X console support, you can't go wrong with the Samsung Odyssey Neo G8. This high-end monitor comes with a 4K, 144Hz VA panel certified with AMD FreeSync Premium and sports an HDMI 2.1 port. It's a great high-end monitor, although it will cost you a high price.

This monitor features the sweet specs you'd expect on a model at this price point: 4K resolution, 144Hz, 2500:1 contrast ratio, HDR 10, and AMD FreeSync Premium. Unlike many high-end monitors, however, the Odyssey Neo G8 uses a VA panel instead of an IPS panel, allowing it to have a super high contrast ratio. However, poorer color consistency across the display and higher response times offset this.

While the Odyssey Neo G8 is an expensive monitor, if you need HDMI 2.1, it's the best option on the market for next-gen support. There are cheaper HDMI 2.1 monitors out there, though, like LG's Ultragear 27GP95R-B and Acer's Nitro XV282K, but they don't offer the expansive 32" screen size of the Odyssey Neo G8.

Picking the best gaming monitor to buy in 2024

There are many options for gaming monitors, but my favorite is the ASUS ROG Swift OLED PG27AQDM. It's pretty expensive, but it has the kind of specs that make it more future-proof than other monitors, and it'll go well with pretty much any PC. A cheaper alternative would be Asus' ROG Strix XG27ACS, which is also 1440p and uses an IPS panel but only goes up to 180Hz (which is still pretty high, in all honesty), and the panel quality is generally a little worse. Still, it's less than half the price and very affordable for most gamers. And if you are building a new setup from scratch, then we have some other collection articles that you might be interested in, including the best mechanical keyboards.

If you're not looking for gaming monitors specifically, then you might want to check out our collection of the best monitors. That list has many more options, including those for productivity. Alternatively, you can join our XDA Computing Forums to discuss your setup or get more product recommendations from the experts in our community.

Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM
Best overall gaming monitor

The ASUS ROG Swift OLED PG27AQDM is the best gaming monitor of 2024, featuring a beautiful OLED panel with a wide range of brightness and individual brightness control settings for each pixel. It also features a sharp 1440p resolution and a super-fast 240Hz refresh rate that can be boosted to 280Hz.

$900 at Amazon $889 at B&H $899 at Asus
item.description:
You have finally managed to buy all the components for your new gaming rig, including a new graphics card. I hope you still have some money left in the budget because now it's time to complete your rig with one of the best gaming monitors. Luckily, there's no shortage of great monitors on the market, so you can pick up the one you want based on the GPU you bought.

There are a few things to consider while shopping for gaming monitors. The one-size-fits-all approach doesn't work here because each user has a different preference. OLED and QD-OLED are taking over as the main panel technology for new gaming monitors, but some users will prefer IPS or VA as fears of OLED burn-in still linger. While some users want a high-refresh-rate panel, others may want to buy a high-resolution monitor for 4K gaming. And still other people give more importance to extra technologies like HDMI 2.1. So, not all of these recommendations might suit your unique needs, but these monitors are worth getting.

  • Source: Asus
    Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM
    Best overall gaming monitor
    $889 at B&H
  • Alienware AW3225QF
    Best 4K gaming monitor
    $1200 at Dell
  • Acer Predator X27U
    Best 1440p monitor
    $730 at Amazon
  • Source: Asus
    Asus ROG Strix XG27ACS
    Best budget 1440p gaming monitor
    $290 at Amazon
  • Source: Dell
    Dell Alienware AW2523HF
    Best 1080p gaming monitor
    $300 at Amazon
  • LG 24GN600-B UltraGear Gaming Monitor
    Best budget 1080p gaming monitor
    $150 at Amazon
  • Source: Dell
    Alienware AW3423DW Gaming Monitor
    Best ultrawide gaming monitor
    $1100 at Amazon
  • Source: Gigabyte
    Gigabyte Aorus FV43U
    Best large gaming monitor
    $710 at Amazon
  • Samsung Odyssey Neo G8 4K UHD Curved Gaming Monitor
    Best gaming monitor with HDMI 2.1
    $847 at Amazon

These are the best gaming monitors in 2024

Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM
Best overall gaming monitor

A monitor with everything you could ask for

The ASUS ROG Swift OLED PG27AQDM is the best gaming monitor of 2024, featuring a beautiful OLED panel with a wide range of brightness and individual brightness control settings for each pixel. It also features a sharp 1440p resolution and a super-fast 240Hz refresh rate that can be boosted to 280Hz.

Pros
  • 1440p, 240Hz refresh rate (via DisplayPort)
  • FreeSync Premium Pro and G-Sync Compatible
  • WOLED panel
Cons
  • Expensive and probably overkill for most people
  • Use of HDMI limits refresh rate to 120Hz
$900 at Amazon $889 at B&H $899 at Asus

The ASUS ROG Swift OLED PG27AQDM is one of the best gaming monitors in 2024, thanks to its stunning OLED panel, ultra-fast response time, and super-speedy refresh rate. The OLED panel delivers a spectacular 1440p resolution with deep blacks, vivid colors, and wide viewing angles. The 2560 x 1440 resolution on a 27-inch OLED panel delivers sharp details and a peak brightness of 1000 nits. HDR content looks stunning on the PG27AQDM. The anti-glare coating is great, and the included stand has a 1/4-inch thread on top for mounting a heavier camera, something content creators will appreciate.

Related
Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM review: For those who crave a gorgeous OLED and high refresh rates for gaming
Among the best OLEDs you can buy.

It's worth mentioning that the HDMI 2.0 ports limit this monitor to a refresh rate of 120Hz, so you'll want to use the DisplayPort 1.4 input to get the 240Hz refresh rate the panel is capable of. The PG27AQDM is great for high visual quality and high framerate gaming. The PG27AQDM features a 0.03ms response time, making it one of the fastest gaming monitors on the market. The monitor can handle fast-paced games without ghosting, blurring, or graphical smearing. That's supported with G-Sync and FreeSync compatibility for blur-free gaming.

The PG27AQDM is an expensive monitor with a $900 MSRP. However, if you are building a mid-range to high-end gaming computer, the PG27AQDM is one of the best monitors thanks to its beautiful OLED panel, 240Hz refresh rate, and excellent HDR support with 1,000 nits of peak brightness.

Alienware AW3225QF
Best 4K gaming monitor

A high-end display best paired with the RTX 4090 and other flagship cards

The Alienware AW3225QF is a powerhouse of gaming performance, with a 32-inch QD-OLED panel with 4K resolution that supports a speedy 240Hz refresh rate. It also supports Dolby Vision, which is a nice bonus for watching Netflix and other streaming services that support the format.

Pros
  • 4K resolution QD-OLED panel with 240Hz refresh rate
  • G-Sync Compatible and VESA AdaptiveSync Certified
  • Excellent HDR performance
Cons
  • Very expensive
$1200 at Dell $1200 at Best Buy

Powerful new graphics cards like the RTX 4090 enable 4K gaming as the norm. If you have one of these powerful GPUs and want to pair it with a high-end 4K display, then I recommend picking up the Alienware AW322QF. It's one of the first 4K QD-OLED panels on the market with a blisteringly fast 240Hz refresh rate and will likely hold up as one of the best gaming monitors through the next generation or two of graphics cards.

Related
Dell Alienware AW3225QF monitor review: Ahead of the curve
The first 4K 32-inch QD-OLED made by one of the most renowned PC monitor makers

While there are more OLED panels on the market now, QD-OLED is still my preferred panel type. That's because it has better color accuracy than WOLED, even if it can't quite manage the same brightness levels. This Alienware monitor has a subtle curve, which isn't strictly necessary on a 32-inch panel, but we found that it distorted screen reflections a bit so that they didn't affect the image as much. This panel is a third-generation QD-OLED, which has a different subpixel arrangement that reduces fringing around horizontal edges, like on text, which is a welcome change.

That reduced fringing will also benefit UI elements in games, and the rest of the panel is superb for gaming use. The 4K resolution and 240Hz refresh rate combine for a crisp, smooth picture, and support for both G-Sync and VESA AdaptiveSync means tear-free gaming. It supports Dolby Vision in supported games and apps and also HDR 400 TrueBlack and HDR Peak 1000 modes for HDR. We found that the panel has a warmer, punchy color balance in SDR on our review unit, which will please most users but may cause issues with color-sensitive work. Its HDR performance is among the best panels we've tested and is a worthy pairing with high-end graphics cards.

Acer Predator X27U
Best 1440p monitor

A high-end 1440p display for high-end PCs

$730 $798 Save $68

The Acer Predator X27U is a premium gaming monitor with stunning visuals, smooth performance, and a zero-frame design. It features a 27-inch OLED panel, a 1440p resolution, 240Hz refresh rate, and HDR 1000 support, delivering lifelike colors.

Pros
  • 1,000 nits peak brightness
  • 1440p OLED panel with 240Hz refresh rate
  • FreeSync Premium Pro
Cons
  • Expensive
  • Poor speaker quality
  • No HDMI 2.1
$730 at Amazon $800 at Acer

While 4K gaming is no longer a distant dream for gamers, most are still leaning towards 1440p gaming thanks to its lower price, lower graphical demand, and higher refresh rate. If you want a great OLED 1440p gaming monitor, check out Acer's Predator X27U.

The Predator X27U sports a 27-inch OLED panel with a 240Hz refresh rate and support for AMD FreeSync Premium Pro. The display has a peak brightness of 1000 nits, providing an excellent OLED gaming experience. Colors appear bright and vivid in HDR content, and are true to realistic coloration.

The Zero-frame design with thin bezels provides an expansive space to game or multitask on, and there's a wide range of connectivity options, whether you prefer to use HDMI, DisplayPort 1.4, or DisplayPort over USB Type-C.

Asus ROG Strix XG27ACS
Best budget 1440p gaming monitor

A cost-effective 1440p gaming monitor

The Asus ROG Strix XG27ACS is a cost-effective way to add a gorgeous, speedy gaming monitor to your setup. The IPS panel might not have stellar HDR performance, but it's perfectly fine for SDR gaming.

Pros
  • 1440p, 180Hz IPS display
  • FreeSync and G-Sync Compatible
  • Well-calibrated from the factory
Cons
  • Weak HDR performance
$290 at Amazon $300 at Best Buy $320 at Newegg

With 1440p still being the sweet spot for PC gaming, it's no surprise to see a 27-inch, 1440p monitor take the budget crown here. The Asus ROG Strix XG27ACS uses a 27-inch IPS panel that's overclocked to a 180Hz refresh rate. That means it's perfect for playing games at high framerates, and support for both AMD FreeSync and Nvidia G-Sync means buttery smooth frames no matter what rate they come at.

Related
Asus ROG Strix XG27ACS review: A gorgeous budget-friendly 1440p gaming monitor
This is all you need for immersive PC gaming in the evenings.

With a fairly subdued gamer styling, this monitor wouldn't look out of place on an office desk. It's got a mostly frameless front with a small bezel on the bottom edge, and comes with a sturdy, adjustable stand. It also features USB-C with power delivery, alongside the DisplayPort and HDMI inputs, which is a rarity on any budget monitor.

The big surprise when we reviewed this monitor was how accurate the factory calibration was. It covered 100% of sRGB, 82% of Adobe RGB, and 91% of DCI P3, which is great for any IPS panel, let alone at this price point. It's worth mentioning that the HDR 400 certification on this panel won't pop as much as on an OLED, mainly because IPS has a very noticeable glow on dark areas. Still, we are talking about a $300 budget monitor here. Asus spent their development budget wisely here, with the resolution, color accuracy, and refresh rate necessary to give you a capable monitor at an affordable price.

Dell Alienware AW2523HF
Best 1080p gaming monitor

The best if you need the highest refresh rate possible

$300 $350 Save $50

The Alienware AW2523HF from Dell is an excellent esport monitor with a 1080p resolution and a super-fast 360Hz refresh rate. This is an ideal monitor for competitive first-person shooters, and is the best option if you want to use max settings and speed.

Pros
  • 360Hz refresh rate
  • Support for AMD FreeSync
  • 25-inch size is a good fit for 1080p
Cons
  • Extremely expensive for a 1080p monitor
  • High refresh rate might not be as important as better resolution
$300 at Amazon $300 at Best Buy

You might think 1080p monitors have been relegated to the low-end, budget part of the market, but that's not entirely true. There's actually a small segment of top-end 1080p gaming monitors that have the highest refresh rates you can get, much higher than the refresh rates on even the best 1440p and 4K panels. Of all the 360Hz monitors you can buy today, Dell's Alienware AW2523HF is the best overall, though it will cost you.

Related
Alienware AW2725DF 27-inch QD-OLED review: Combining great color accuracy with 360Hz refresh rates
There are plenty of high refresh rate or color-accurate OLED gaming monitors out there, but it's rare to see both characteristics on the same display.

The AW2523HF goes for the standard minimalist look many monitors have these days: thin bezels at the sides and top, slightly thicker chin at the bottom, and an all-black plastic theme. It's an unassuming monitor that certainly doesn't look eye-catching, although that's not really a problem when it's the panel that's supposed to look good and not what surrounds it.

Speaking of the panel, it measures 25 inches across and is rated at 360Hz. The AW2523HF comes with support for AMD FreeSync and is G-Sync Compatible, which pretty much has to be on a monitor that sells itself on its refresh rate. Its peak brightness of around 400 nits is decent enough, though out-of-the-box reviews indicate that its color accuracy isn't amazing, and it doesn't have any HDR certification either.

Although it's our top pick in this segment, Dell's Alienware AW2523HF is undeniably in a niche within a niche. A 1080p monitor, even with a 360Hz refresh rate, is expensive at $300+. However, the AW2523HF is really made for games like Valorant and Overwatch, and not so much for gaming in general. If you have the money and don't plan on playing all your games at over 300FPS, you should probably consider HP's X27q or Acer's Nitro XV272U, which are 1440p gaming monitors that cost less.

LG 24GN600-B UltraGear Gaming Monitor
Best budget 1080p gaming monitor

Cheap but still good for gaming

$150 $200 Save $50

LG's 24GN600-B UltaGear is an excellent entry-level gaming monitor. Measuring at 24-inches, this 1080p monitor has a refresh rate of 144 Hz and FreeSync Premium support for a tear-free gaming experience. While it's not the biggest monitor, it's one of the best and most affordable 1080p gaming monitors.

Pros
  • 1080p IPS display
  • 144Hz refresh rate
  • AMD FreeSync Premium
Cons
  • No explicit support for G-Sync (though it should still function)
$150 at Amazon $150 at LG

In recent years, entry-level gaming monitors have gotten better while prices remain about as low as they've always been. It's not uncommon to find a 1080p display with a high refresh rate for less than $200, but LG's 24GN600-B UltraGear manages to stand out from the crowd by offering tons of features and impressive specs for just under $200 (with frequent sales bringing it under $150).

From the front, the 24GN600-B is a pretty normal-looking 24-inch monitor. It has a thin, pure black bezel made of plastic decorated only by the LG logo. On the back, however, this monitor can only be described as gamer-y thanks to its purely decorative wheel-like image with red accents. The back of the monitor stand is also pure red. It's kind of weird that all the flashy esthetics are put in the one area where you're the least likely to be looking, but that might work out if you prefer the basic appearance of this monitor from the front anyway.

On a technical level, the 24GN600-B has quite an impressive set of features. 1080p and a 144Hz refresh rate is nothing special these days, but this is also an IPS display with AMD FreeSync Premium. That basically ensures good color accuracy (LG says this monitor hits 99% of the sRGB color space) and good enough latency for gaming. For $150, you're getting a high refresh rate, decent color accuracy, and limited to no screen tearing, no matter what GPU you're using, since all three major vendors support FreeSync. It's hard to ask for anything more.

There are other contenders in the sub-$200 segment, but LG's 24GN600-B UltraGear checks off the most boxes for the least amount of money. Asus's TUF Gaming VG27VH1B is one such alternative, which bumps up the screen size to 27 inches, the refresh rate up to 165Hz, and it has a curved display. However, the 24GN600-B goes on sale more frequently and for lower prices than the VG27VH1B, which makes it a generally better deal as long as you can wait for LG's monitor to hit that expected $150 price point.

Alienware AW3423DW Gaming Monitor
Best ultrawide gaming monitor

A top-of-the-line ultrawide display for gaming

The Alienware AW3423DW Gaming Monitor is one of the best 1440p ultra-wide monitors, featuring a 1440p resolution, 175Hz refresh rate, and support for both Nvidia G-Sync and AMD FreeSync, so you'll have a tear-free experience no matter which GPU you have.

Pros
  • 1440p and 175Hz refresh rate
  • G-Sync and FreeSync support
  • Fantastic HDR experience
Cons
  • Extremely expensive
$1100 at Amazon $1100 at Dell

Ultrawide monitors are the realm for gamers who want to make full use of their peripheral vision. Gaming at an ultrawide resolution can be buggy at times and requires more graphical horsepower than the typical 16:9 aspect ratio, but the visual experience has proven to be worth those issues. Of all the ultrawide monitors out there, Dell's Alienware AW3423DW is certainly one of the best, though it's not exactly easy on the wallet.

From the front, the AW3423DW presents its thin bezels and almost pure black design, making it look like a pretty normal monitor. On the back, however, there's quite a bit of visual flair; the black largely gives way to white, and there's some RGB around where the monitor stand meets the monitor itself. However, what a monitor looks like from the back isn't always important, especially if you have this monitor up against a wall or something.

The AW3423DW has a top-end Quantum Dot OLED 1440p panel with a 175Hz refresh rate. This is also an 1800R curved display, which is sometimes not desirable on typical gaming monitors but is often very appreciated on ultrawide models like this one. The AW3423DW supports G-Sync (not G-Sync Compatible but actual G-Sync) and AMD FreeSync, which means no screen tearing in games, no matter what GPU you have. One weakness of this monitor is its relatively low SDR brightness of 250 nits, but in HDR content, the brightness is allowed to hit values closer to 1000 nits. It's worth mentioning that firmware updates are now possible to do at home by the user, whereas they used to require sending the monitor back to Dell to be updated.

Dell's Alienware AW3423DW is undeniably one of the best ultrawide gaming monitors out there, but it's also one of the most expensive monitors overall. Cheaper alternatives include Samsung's SJ55W, which is also 1440p and $300 but is limited to 75Hz, Gigabyte's GG34WQC A, which offers 1440p and 144Hz for $400, and Dell's non-Alienware S3422DWG, which is also around $400 for a 1440p, 144Hz panel. These displays are all, of course, inferior in quality to the AW3423DW, which is the best as long as you can afford it.

Gigabyte Aorus FV43U
Best large gaming monitor

Great for the living room

$710 $750 Save $40

Gigabyte's Aorus FV43U is a TV-sized monitor measuring 40-inches in a VA panel, and provides a crisp 4K resolution, excellent contrast, and a fast 144Hz refresh rate. If you are looking for a large format monitor, the Gigabyte AORUS FV43U is the best without breaking the bank.

Pros
  • 43 inches
  • 4K resolution
  • 144Hz refresh rate
Cons
  • VA panels are a bit outdated
$710 at Amazon

While most people don't lean towards buying monitors bigger than 32 inches, there are people who like huge monitors for their gaming setups. If you are one of them, then you'd be interested in checking out the Gigabyte Aorus FV43U. This monitor is essentially a TV replacement, packing a massive 43-inch panel; however, it doesn't have typical TV functions like channel switching, and it doesn't even come with a remote. But you can sit close and enjoy content or play games on it. It also has a good amount of height thanks to the 16:9 aspect ratio, so it's a very different experience to using an ultrawide.

The Gigabyte Aorus FV43U comes with a 43-inch 4K VA with a 144Hz refresh rate and 1ms response time, which means it's great for gaming. In addition to that, the Gigabyte Aorus FV43U also has support for FreeSync, so you can get rid of those nasty tears on AMD Radeon-powered systems, though it should also work with Nvidia GPUs with some manual driver option selections.

In terms of image quality, the Gigabyte Aorus FV43U handles things very well too. It offers 300 nits of brightness typically and a 4000:1 contrast ratio, which makes up for some of the typical weaknesses of a VA display. It also comes with support for DisplayHDR 1000 and HDR10 certification. Those who are looking to use this monitor with the next-generation consoles, including Xbox Series X, S, and PS5, will also have a great time as it sports HDMI 2.1 ports.

If you're focused on size or a display that has great gaming features for the living room, then Gigabyte's Aorus FV43U would be an awesome choice. There aren't a ton of TV-sized gaming monitors out there to begin with, so the FV43U doesn't necessarily win by default and also has lots of things going for it like its decently high 144Hz refresh rate and 4K resolution.

Samsung Odyssey Neo G8 4K UHD Curved Gaming Monitor
Best gaming monitor with HDMI 2.1

If you need HDMI 2.1 in particular

$847 $1300 Save $453

Samsung's 32-inch Odyssey Neo G8 4K UHD Curved Gaming Monitor is one of the best monitor's on the market if you can afford it. It features multiple DisplayPort and HDMI 2.1 ports so that you can enjoy not only PC gaming but also PlayStation 5 and Xbox Series X gaming on this monitor.

Pros
  • Has HDMI 2.1
  • 4K VA panel
  • 144Hz refresh rate with FreeSync Premium
Cons
  • Fairly expensive
$847 at Amazon $1300 at Samsung $900 at Newegg $1300 at Best Buy

If you're looking for a monitor with HDMI 2.1 for PlayStation 5 and Xbox Series X console support, you can't go wrong with the Samsung Odyssey Neo G8. This high-end monitor comes with a 4K, 144Hz VA panel certified with AMD FreeSync Premium and sports an HDMI 2.1 port. It's a great high-end monitor, although it will cost you a high price.

This monitor features the sweet specs you'd expect on a model at this price point: 4K resolution, 144Hz, 2500:1 contrast ratio, HDR 10, and AMD FreeSync Premium. Unlike many high-end monitors, however, the Odyssey Neo G8 uses a VA panel instead of an IPS panel, allowing it to have a super high contrast ratio. However, poorer color consistency across the display and higher response times offset this.

While the Odyssey Neo G8 is an expensive monitor, if you need HDMI 2.1, it's the best option on the market for next-gen support. There are cheaper HDMI 2.1 monitors out there, though, like LG's Ultragear 27GP95R-B and Acer's Nitro XV282K, but they don't offer the expansive 32" screen size of the Odyssey Neo G8.

Picking the best gaming monitor to buy in 2024

There are many options for gaming monitors, but my favorite is the ASUS ROG Swift OLED PG27AQDM. It's pretty expensive, but it has the kind of specs that make it more future-proof than other monitors, and it'll go well with pretty much any PC. A cheaper alternative would be Asus' ROG Strix XG27ACS, which is also 1440p and uses an IPS panel but only goes up to 180Hz (which is still pretty high, in all honesty), and the panel quality is generally a little worse. Still, it's less than half the price and very affordable for most gamers. And if you are building a new setup from scratch, then we have some other collection articles that you might be interested in, including the best mechanical keyboards.

If you're not looking for gaming monitors specifically, then you might want to check out our collection of the best monitors. That list has many more options, including those for productivity. Alternatively, you can join our XDA Computing Forums to discuss your setup or get more product recommendations from the experts in our community.

Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM
Best overall gaming monitor

The ASUS ROG Swift OLED PG27AQDM is the best gaming monitor of 2024, featuring a beautiful OLED panel with a wide range of brightness and individual brightness control settings for each pixel. It also features a sharp 1440p resolution and a super-fast 240Hz refresh rate that can be boosted to 280Hz.

$900 at Amazon $889 at B&H $899 at Asus
